title: Modern Farm Table (Pocket Hole Plan)
summary: A 1x6-topped farm table with a 2x4 apron frame and chunky 2x6 legs, built entirely with pocket-hole joinery.
description:
This is a farm table built the modern way — pocket holes instead of mortise-and-tenon — which means the whole base assembles quickly, but the tabletop is still where the real craftsmanship shows. Clamp and join the six tabletop boards carefully, working for the smoothest possible surface, and notch the layout so the 2x6 legs actually fit where they need to once the base goes on.

Test-fit the base against the tabletop before final assembly — the inside apron supports may need small length adjustments to land exactly where you want them, and that's much easier to catch dry-fit than after everything is screwed together.

tools.note: Clamps the tabletop boards tight while joining, for the smoothest surface.

cutList.note: Cut to fit — dry-fit against your base before cutting to length.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut four tabletop boards, long at 72" and two tabletop boards, short (ends) at 69" from the 1x6. Cut two side aprons at 69", three inside supports at 30" and two leg braces at 22" from the 2x4. Cut four legs at 30" from the 2x6. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to six 6 ft 1x6, three 8 ft 2x4 and one 12 ft 2x6. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build the tabletop
steps.body:
Drill 3/4" pocket holes along one long edge of all but one of the short tabletop boards, spaced about every 6–8". Clamp and join the boards, working carefully for the smoothest possible surface — you'll need the 2x6 legs to fit into the notched-out areas of the assembled top, so keep that layout in mind as you join.

If your boards are exactly 72" long but have rough ends, consider making the whole table 1/2" shorter so you have room to trim the ends clean.

steps.title: Build and attach the base
steps.body:
Test-fit the base boards first to make sure the assembled base will match the width of the tabletop — you may need to adjust the length of the inside supports.

Drill 3/4" pocket holes along one long edge of the side aprons and inside supports, spaced every 6–8", for attaching to the tabletop later. Drill two 1-1/2" pocket holes in each end of the inside supports and attach them to the side aprons with 2-1/2" pocket-hole screws, keeping the 3/4" holes facing upward and hidden on the inside.

Attach the whole base to the tabletop through the 3/4" pocket holes with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws.

steps.title: Build and attach the legs
steps.body: Build the leg sets first, then glue and screw them to the frame from the inside.

images.alt: Modern farm table with a 1x6 top and chunky 2x6 legs
